# n8n “undefined” Errors — Why They Happen (and How to Fix Them)

## Most n8n “undefined” errors aren’t random — they happen when your data doesn’t match your assumptions.

[![Ricardo Landeros](https://miro.medium.com/v2/da:true/resize:fill:32:32/0*jOBHKAazyUtqdP6S)](https://medium.com/@landerosr81?source=post_page---byline--d4299a4cfcb6---------------------------------------)

[Ricardo Landeros](https://medium.com/@landerosr81?source=post_page---byline--d4299a4cfcb6---------------------------------------)

Follow

4 min read

·

May 2, 2026

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Dd4299a4cfcb6&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40landerosr81%2Fn8n-undefined-errors-why-they-happen-and-how-to-fix-them-d4299a4cfcb6&source=---header_actions--d4299a4cfcb6---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

You’re building a workflow, everything looks correct, and then suddenly:

**“undefined.”**

## Get Ricardo Landeros’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

No clear error. No obvious cause. Just a value that isn’t there.

If you’ve searched for things like “n8n undefined error” or “n8n expression undefined,” you’re not alone. This is one of the most common issues people run into when building workflows in n8n — and one of the most confusing to debug.

## The real problem: your data doesn’t match your assumptions

Most n8n workflows don’t fail because of bad logic. They fail because the data moving between nodes isn’t consistent. When you see “undefined,” it usually means your workflow is trying to access something that doesn’t exist in that specific run. Not because it never exists — but because it doesn’t always exist.

## A real example: form submissions

On one run, your form submission might include everything:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*dKTURjCiWl09NFujSpHC2Q.png)

## On one run, the incoming form data might include the item ID:

```
{
  "email": "customer@example.com",
  "plantItem": "SPID-7782"
}
```

So the Code node builds the API path:

```
/api/plants/{{$json.plantItem}}
```

That works.

## But on another run, the form submission might look like this:

```
{
  "email": "customer@example.com",
  "itemName": "Monstera"
}
```

Now the workflow is still trying to use:

```
{{$json.plantItem}}
```

But `plantItem` doesn’t exist in the incoming data, so the expression resolves to:

```
undefined
```

That means the HTTP Request node may end up trying to call something like:

```
/api/plants/undefined
```

That’s why the workflow fails even though the nodes themselves look connected correctly. The issue is not the workflow structure — it’s that the data the Code or HTTP Request node expected was missing.

## Another example: API responses that vary

You request order data from an API. Sometimes you get a nested customer object with an email inside it. Other times — like guest checkouts — the email is returned as a top-level field with a different name.

If your workflow always expects the same path, it will fail whenever the structure changes. The data is still there, but your expression is pointing to the wrong place.

## **Another common case: branching and merges**

After IF nodes or merges, your data can look different depending on which path ran. One branch might include additional fields, while another doesn’t.

If you later assume those fields exist everywhere, your expressions will occasionally return undefined. These are some of the hardest issues to spot because the workflow appears valid, but only breaks under certain conditions.

## **Why this keeps happening**

You build your workflow based on one successful run, then assume the data will always look the same. In reality, forms are incomplete, APIs vary, and branching logic creates multiple possible data shapes.

Over time, those differences surface as undefined values.

## A simple way to debug it

Instead of guessing, isolate the problem. Run the workflow only up to the failing node, then inspect what the previous step actually returned. Focus on the structure of the data, not just the values. Check whether the field exists, whether it’s nested differently, or whether the name has changed.

Once you see what’s really there, update your expression accordingly and test again with a few different inputs. If it works across multiple runs, you’ve likely fixed the root issue.

## Small changes that prevent this

A few habits make a big difference. Check that fields exist before using them, normalize data early so everything downstream follows the same structure, and be careful when merging branches or handling optional inputs. These don’t eliminate every issue, but they make your workflows far more reliable.

## When debugging gets repetitive

If you’ve ever stepped through node outputs over and over trying to find where something disappeared, you know how slow this can get.

At that point, the challenge isn’t just fixing the error — it’s figuring out where your assumption broke. Tools that analyze workflow output and highlight likely causes can speed this up significantly. For example, [Debugalo](https://debugalo.com/) helps break down n8n errors by analyzing the output and pointing to what likely changed in the data, so you don’t have to trace everything manually.

## The takeaway

“Undefined” isn’t a bug in n8n. It’s a signal. It tells you your workflow expected something that isn’t guaranteed to be there. Once you shift from asking “Why did this break?” to “What changed in the data?”, these issues become much easier to understand — and fix.

## Describe the problem/error/question

I have a presistent issue between Airtable and N8N. I have added a new Single line/ Multiline Text field. Using the Airtable Node I'd able to excute the step and find the field in Airtable. But since its a new field, its emplty for all records. Until I add placeholder text in any of the records for this field, on the input side nothing is displayed, BUT after I add the data, the field is found on the input but when you use that as a condition or routing rule that field and only that field comes through Red or undefined. I was able to find a work around yesterday changing the IF node to a SWITCH node, but now see the issue using the SWITCH Node.

Undefined
